---
title: "Cameron & Trivedi -- Ch. 16: Tobit and selection models (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Colin Cameron
- Pravin Trivedi
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
const DATA = let c = filter(d -> isdir(d) && (isfile(joinpath(d, "auto.dta")) || isdir(joinpath(d, "musr"))), ["../../data", "../data", "data"]); isempty(c) ? error("StatEcon example data not found; run this notebook from its own folder (examples/Cameron_Trivedi) or the repo root.") : first(c) end
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq
_rawval(x) = hasproperty(x, :value) ? x.value : x
```

The censored-regression and sample-selection estimators used in this chapter
now live in `StatEcon` and are called directly below:

* `stata_tobit`                    — `tobit y x, ll(#) ul(#)` (canonical Tobit MLE)
* `stata_margins_tobit`            — `margins, dydx(*) predict(...)` after `tobit`
* `stata_predict_tobit_lognormal`  — lognormal-tobit level predictions
* `stata_heckman`                  — `heckman y x, select(d = z)` (FIML)
* `stata_heckman_twostep`          — `heckman ..., twostep` (Heckman 1979)
* `stata_predict_heckman`          — `heckman` post-estimation predictions
* `stata_sktest`                   — `sktest var` (skewness/kurtosis normality test)
* `stata_ols_fit`                  — OLS returning residuals + `e(ll)`

The workhorse commands `stata_probit`, `stata_regress`, `stata_summarize`,
`stata_tabulate` and `estat_hettest` (Breusch-Pagan / Cook-Weisberg) are also
in `StatEcon`. The `estat_hettest` default variant is the OLS-residual
Breusch-Pagan test against the fitted values, exactly the statistic the
chapter's `hettest` produces. The by-hand demonstrations (direct marginal
effects, generalized residuals, the LM tests of normality and
homoskedasticity, and the two-part-model predictions) are kept inline as
pedagogical scaffolding.

# 16 Tobit and selection models

## 16.1 Introduction

## 16.2 Tobit model

### 16.2.1 Regression with censored data

### 16.2.2 Tobit model setup

### 16.2.3 Unknown censoring point

### 16.2.4 Tobit estimation

### 16.2.5 ML estimation in Stata

## 16.3 Tobit model example

### 16.3.1 Data summary

The data come from Deb, Munkin and Trivedi (2006), "Bayesian Analysis of
Two-Part Model with Endogeneity", *Journal of Applied Econometrics*. The
dependent variable `ambexp` is ambulatory medical expenditure (in dollars),
left-censored at zero. Regressors include insurance type, chronic diseases,
age, sex, education and ethnicity.

```{julia}
#=
********** DATA DESCRIPTION **********

* Subset of data in P. Deb, M. Munkin and P.K. Trivedi (2006)
* "Bayesian Analysis of Two-Part Model with Endogeneity", Journal of Applied Econometrics, 21, 1081-1100
* Only the data for year 2001 are used

* Dependent variable is
*      ambexp   Ambulatory medical expenditures (excluding dental and outpatient mental)
*      lambexp  Ln(ambexp) given ambexp > 0 ; missing otherwise
*      dambexp  1 if ambexp > 0 and 0 otherwise
*      lnambexp  ln(ambexp) if ambexp>0 and 0 if ambexp=0

* Regressors are
*  - Health insurance measures
*       ins either PPO or HMO type insurance
*  - Health status measures
*       totchr     number of chronic diseases
*  - Socioeconomic characteristics
*       age     age in years/10
*       female   1 for females, zero otherwise
*       educ     years of schooling of decision maker
*       blhisp   either black or hispanic
=#
```

```{julia}
#=
* Raw data summary
use musr/mus16data.dta, clear
summarize ambexp age female educ blhisp totchr ins
=#

# Load mus16data (Deb-Munkin-Trivedi ambulatory-expenditure panel) and
# call `stata_summarize` on the seven variables Stata lists.
df16 = DataFrames.DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("$(DATA)/musr/mus16data.dta"))
for c in DataFrames.names(df16)
    if eltype(df16[!, c]) <: Union{Missing, Float32}
        df16[!, c] = convert.(Union{Missing, Float64}, df16[!, c])
    end
end
stata_summarize(df16, [:ambexp, :age, :female, :educ, :blhisp, :totchr, :ins]);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Detailed summary to show skewness and kurtosis
summarize ambexp, detail
=#

# `stata_summarize` with `detail = true` reproduces Stata's percentile /
# Smallest / Largest / moments block.
stata_summarize(df16, [:ambexp]; detail = true);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Summary for positives only
summarize ambexp if ambexp >0, detail
=#

# Stata's `if ambexp > 0` filter maps to a `DataFrames.filter` on df16.
stata_summarize(DataFrames.filter(:ambexp => >(0), df16),
                [:ambexp]; detail = true);
```

### 16.3.2 Tobit analysis

```{julia}
#=
* Tobit analysis for ambexp using all expenditures
global xlist age female educ blhisp totchr ins   //Define regressor list $xlist
tobit ambexp $xlist, ll(0)
=#

# `stata_tobit` fits a Tobit MLE left-censored at 0 (Stata's `ll(0)`) by
# L-BFGS + ForwardDiff gradient; OIM vcov via FD Hessian with delta-method
# on σ = exp(lnσ). The returned NamedTuple binds `β`, `σ`, `X`, `coefnames`
# so the §16.3.3 / .4 prediction / marginal-effect chunks can read them.
tobit_res = stata_tobit(df16, :ambexp,
    [:age, :female, :educ, :blhisp, :totchr, :ins]; ll = 0.0)
β_tob   = tobit_res.β
σ_tob   = tobit_res.σ
X16     = tobit_res.X
xnms16  = tobit_res.coefnames;
```

### 16.3.3 Prediction after tobit

```{julia}
#=
* Tobit prediction and summary
predict yhatlin
summarize yhatlin
=#

# Stata's bare `predict <name>` after `tobit` returns the linear prediction
# xβ (the latent index — no censoring adjustment). We compute `X16 * β_tob`.
yhatlin = X16 * β_tob
stata_summarize(DataFrames.DataFrame(yhatlin = yhatlin), [:yhatlin]);
```

### 16.3.4 Marginal effects

#### Censored / uncensored / truncated expectations

```{julia}
#=
** Tobit postestimation
* (1) ME on censored expected value E(y|x,y>0)
quietly tobit ambexp age i.female educ i.blhisp totchr i.ins, ll(0)
margins, dydx(*) predict(e(0,.)) atmean noatlegend
=#

# `stata_margins_tobit` — Stata's `margins, dydx(*) predict(e(0, .)) atmean`.
# `predict = :e_cond` selects the truncated conditional expectation
# `E[y | L < y < U, x]` with L/U defaulting to the fit's censoring bounds
# (here lower = 0, upper = ∞). SE is delta-method.
stata_margins_tobit(tobit_res; predict = :e_cond, at = :mean);
```

```{julia}
#=
* (2) ME without censoring on E(y|x)
margins, dydx(*) predict(ystar(0,.)) atmean noatlegend
=#

# `predict = :e_uncond` is the censored unconditional expectation
# `E[y* | x]` where `y* = max(L, min(y, U))`. Defaults to the fit's
# bounds (lower = 0, upper = ∞ here); SE is delta-method.
stata_margins_tobit(tobit_res; predict = :e_uncond, at = :mean);
```

```{julia}
#=
* (3) ME when E(y|0<y<535)
margins, dydx(*) predict(e(0,535)) atmean noatlegend
=#

# Same helper, now with `upper = 535` for the doubly-truncated
# conditional expectation `E[y | 0 < y < 535]`.
stata_margins_tobit(tobit_res; predict = :e_cond, at = :mean,
                    lower = 0.0, upper = 535.0);
```

#### Left-censored case computed directly

```{julia}
#=
* Direct computation of marginal effects for E(y|x)
predict xb1, xb // xb1 is estimate of x'b
matrix btobit = e(b)
scalar sigma = btobit[1,11] // sigma is estimate of sigma
matrix bcoeff = btobit[1,1..9] // bcoeff is betas excl. constant
quietly summarize xb1
scalar meanxb = r(mean) // mean of x'b equals (mean of x)'b
scalar PHI = normal(meanxb/sigma)
matrix deriv = PHI*bcoeff
matrix list deriv
=#

# Direct closed-form ∂E[y|x]/∂x_j = Φ(meanxb/σ) · β_j, in the explicit
# element-by-element form Stata's `matrix list deriv` shows. Produces the
# same numbers as `stata_margins_tobit(...; predict = :e_uncond, at = :mean)`.
xb1     = X16 * β_tob
meanxb  = Statistics.mean(xb1)
PHI     = cdf(Normal(), meanxb / σ_tob)
deriv   = PHI .* β_tob[1:end - 1]
Printf.@printf("\nmeanxb = %.4f   σ = %.4f   Φ(meanxb/σ) = %.6f\n\n",
               meanxb, σ_tob, PHI)
println("deriv[1, 1..$(length(deriv))]:")
for (n, d) in zip(xnms16[1:end - 1], deriv)
    Printf.@printf("%-10s  %12.6f\n", n, d)
end
```

```{julia}
#=
* The following gives nicer looking results
ereturn post deriv
ereturn display
=#

# Stata's `ereturn post deriv; ereturn display` reposts `deriv` as e(b) and
# prints it through the coefficient-table renderer (no SE for a bare vector).
println()
println("-"^60)
Printf.@printf("%12s | %12s\n", "", "Coefficient")
println("-"^13, "+", "-"^46)
for (n, d) in zip(xnms16[1:end - 1], deriv)
    Printf.@printf("%12s | %12.6f\n", n, d)
end
println("-"^60)
```

#### Marginal impact on probabilities

```{julia}
#=
* Compute margins on Pr(5000<ambexp<10000)
quietly tobit ambexp age i.female educ i.blhisp totchr i.ins, ll(0) vce(robust)
margins, dydx(*) predict(pr(5000,10000)) atmean noatlegend
=#

# Same helper, with `predict = :pr` and the interval (5000, 10000). Stata's
# closed-form ∂P(L < y < U)/∂x_c = (β_c/σ)·[φ((L-η)/σ) − φ((U-η)/σ)]. The
# Stata refit uses vce(robust) SEs; our helper reports OIM SEs.
stata_margins_tobit(tobit_res; predict = :pr, at = :mean,
                    lower = 5000.0, upper = 10000.0);
```

### 16.3.5 IV tobit

### 16.3.6 Other censored regression options

## 16.4 Tobit for lognormal data

### 16.4.1 Data example

The dependent variable `lny` is `ln(ambexp)` if positive, with the censored
zeros mapped to a small value `γ - ε` to keep them on the same scale.

```{julia}
#=
* ASIDE:
* We observe  y = 0       if dy=0   and  y = y  if dy=1
* Let gamma be the smallest of the positive y's
* We define lny = gamma-0.000001 if dy=0   and lny = ln(y) if dy=1
* This ensures that tobit, ll will correctly sort out
* the censored and uncensored observations

* Summary of log(expenditures) for positives only
summarize lambexp, detail
=#

# `lambexp` is pre-computed as ln(ambexp) for positive expenditures (missing
# for ambexp == 0); drop the missings and summarize with `detail = true`.
stata_summarize(DataFrames.dropmissing(df16, :lambexp),
                [:lambexp]; detail = true);
```

### 16.4.2 Setting the censoring point for the log model

```{julia}
#=
* "Tricking" Stata to handle log transformation
generate y = ambexp
generate dy = ambexp > 0
generate lny = ln(y)                // Zero values will become missing
quietly summarize lny
scalar gamma = r(min)               // This could be negative
display "gamma = " gamma
replace lny = gamma - 0.0000001 if lny == .
tabulate y if y < 0.02              // .02 is arbitrary small value
tabulate lny if lny < gamma + 0.02
=#

# Recipe to map log-of-positives onto a tobit-friendly censored variable:
# zeros get a value just below the smallest log(positive) (`γ - 1e-7`), so a
# left-censored tobit at `γ` recovers the zero/positive split correctly.
df16.y   = Float64.(_rawval.(df16.ambexp))
df16.dy  = Int.(df16.y .> 0)
df16.lny = [yi > 0 ? log(yi) : missing for yi in df16.y]
γ_amb    = minimum(skipmissing(df16.lny))
Printf.@printf("gamma = %.10f\n", γ_amb)
df16.lny = [ismissing(v) ? γ_amb - 1e-7 : v for v in df16.lny]

# `tabulate y if y < 0.02` — near-zero frequency (collapses to a count of
# y == 0, the censored obs).
stata_tabulate(DataFrames.filter(:y => <(0.02), df16), :y)
# `tabulate lny if lny < gamma + 0.02` — the imputed `γ − 1e-7` value with a
# count equal to the number of zero-expenditure obs.
stata_tabulate(DataFrames.filter(:lny => <(γ_amb + 0.02), df16), :lny);
```

```{julia}
#=
// Not included in book
summarize y lny dy $xlist
summarize y lny dy, detail
=#

# `stata_summarize` — first the basic block over y, lny, dy + the xlist,
# then the `, detail` block for each of y, lny, dy.
stata_summarize(df16, [:y, :lny, :dy,
                       :age, :female, :educ, :blhisp, :totchr, :ins])
stata_summarize(df16, [:y, :lny, :dy]; detail = true);
```

### 16.4.3 Results

```{julia}
#=
* Now do tobit on lny and calculate threshold and lambda
tobit lny $xlist, ll
=#

# Stata's pasted output here is an **intercept-only** tobit — the `$xlist`
# global was never set in the textbook's session, so `tobit lny $xlist, ll`
# collapsed to `tobit lny, ll`. Stata's `ll` (no argument) counts obs at the
# limit as uncensored, so no censoring binds and the fit is OLS on lny with
# an intercept. We reproduce that with an intercept-only model and `ll` set
# below the data so nothing is censored.
tobit_lny_res = stata_tobit(df16, :lny, Symbol[];
    ll = minimum(df16.lny) - 1.0)
β_tob_lny = tobit_lny_res.β
σ_tob_lny = tobit_lny_res.σ
# `var(e.lny)` in Stata is the biased ML variance — divides by N, not N - 1.
N = tobit_lny_res.n
var_mle = σ_tob_lny^2
var_unbiased = var_mle * N / (N - 1)
Printf.@printf("\nThreshold (lower bound)  ll = %.4f\n", tobit_lny_res.ll_bound)
Printf.@printf("Mean (_cons)               = %.6f\n", β_tob_lny[end])
Printf.@printf("σ̂ (MLE)                    = %.6f\n", σ_tob_lny)
Printf.@printf("var(e.lny)  (= σ̂²)         = %.6f\n", var_mle)
```

```{julia}
#=
* OLS, not tobit
regress lny $xlist, noheader
=#

# Stata's pasted output is again intercept-only (`$xlist` undefined). For an
# intercept-only regression β̂ = mean(lny), SE = std(lny)/√N, t = β̂ / SE.
let
    y = collect(skipmissing(df16.lny))
    n = length(y)
    μ̂ = Statistics.mean(y)
    σ̂ = Statistics.std(y)            # sample std (N-1 denom)
    se = σ̂ / sqrt(n)
    t  = μ̂ / se
    crit = quantile(TDist(n - 1), 0.975)
    lo  = μ̂ - crit * se
    hi  = μ̂ + crit * se
    println("-"^78)
    Printf.@printf("%12s | Coefficient  Std. err.      t    P>|t|     [95%% conf. interval]\n",
                   "lny")
    println("-"^13, "+", "-"^64)
    p_val = 2 * (1 - cdf(TDist(n - 1), abs(t)))
    Printf.@printf("%12s | %10.6f  %9.7f  %6.2f   %.3f    %8.6f   %8.6f\n",
                   "_cons", μ̂, se, t, p_val, lo, hi)
    println("-"^78)
end;
```

### 16.4.4 Two-limit tobit

```{julia}
#=
* Now do two-limit tobit
scalar upper = log(10000)
display upper
tobit lny $xlist, ll ul(9.2103404)
=#

# `stata_tobit` supports both lower and upper censoring bounds. As above the
# pasted Stata output is intercept-only with Stata's `Left-censored = 0`
# quirk (obs at the lower limit treated as uncensored). We mimic that by
# fitting intercept-only with only the upper bound active — `ll = nothing`
# skips left-censoring, `ul = log(10000)` right-clips the obs at ambexp ≥ 10000.
upper = log(10000)
Printf.@printf("upper = log(10000) = %.7f\n", upper)
tobit_2lim_res = stata_tobit(df16, :lny, Symbol[];
    ll = nothing, ul = upper);
```

### 16.4.5 Model diagnostics

### 16.4.6 Tests of normality and homoskedasticity

#### Generalized residuals

```{julia}
#=
* Mills' ratio
quietly tobit lny $xlist, ll
predict xb, xb                            // xb is estimate of x'b
matrix btobit = e(b)
scalar sigma = btobit[1,e(df_m)+2]        // sigma is estimate of sigma
generate threshold = (gamma-xb)/sigma     // gamma: lower censoring point
generate lambda = normalden(threshold)/normal(threshold)

* Generalized residuals (gres1..gres4). gres1, gres2 have mean zero by the
* first-order conditions; gres3, gres4 have mean zero if correctly specified.
quietly generate uifdyeq1 = (lny - xb)/sigma if dy == 1
quietly generate double gres1 = uifdyeq1
quietly replace gres1 = -lambda if dy == 0
summarize gres1
=#

# Tobit generalized-residual `gres1` (Gourieroux et al. 1987). With `$xlist`
# undefined in Stata, the underlying tobit is intercept-only — reuse
# `tobit_lny_res` from §16.4.3.
#
# Subtle: Stata's `scalar sigma = btobit[1,e(df_m)+2]` reads the
# second-from-last entry of e(b), which in newer Stata is `var(e.lny)` — the
# *variance* — not σ̂. So `threshold = (gamma-xb)/sigma` actually uses σ̂², and
# `gres1` for positives is `(lny − xb) / σ̂²`. We follow the same substitution.
xb          = tobit_lny_res.X * tobit_lny_res.β
σ_lny       = tobit_lny_res.σ
sigma_buggy = σ_lny^2                              # what Stata reads
threshold   = (γ_amb .- xb) ./ sigma_buggy
λ_tobit     = [pdf(Normal(), z) / cdf(Normal(), z) for z in threshold]
gres1       = [df16.dy[i] == 1 ? (df16.lny[i] - xb[i]) / sigma_buggy :
                                 -λ_tobit[i]
               for i in 1:DataFrames.nrow(df16)]
df16.gres1 = gres1
stata_summarize(df16, [:gres1]);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Second- to fourth-sample moments
quietly generate double gres2 = uifdyeq1^2 - 1
quietly replace gres2 = -threshold*lambda if dy == 0
quietly generate double gres3 = uifdyeq1^3
replace gres3 = -(2 + threshold^2)*lambda if dy == 0
quietly generate double gres4 = uifdyeq1^4 - 3
quietly replace gres4 = -(3*threshold + threshold^3)*lambda if dy == 0
=#

# Higher-order generalized residuals (Gourieroux et al.). `sigma` follows the
# buggy textbook substitution from the previous chunk (= σ̂², not σ̂).
uifdyeq1 = (df16.lny .- xb) ./ sigma_buggy
df16.gres2 = [df16.dy[i] == 1 ? uifdyeq1[i]^2 - 1 :
                                -threshold[i] * λ_tobit[i]
              for i in 1:DataFrames.nrow(df16)]
df16.gres3 = [df16.dy[i] == 1 ? uifdyeq1[i]^3 :
                                -(2 + threshold[i]^2) * λ_tobit[i]
              for i in 1:DataFrames.nrow(df16)]
df16.gres4 = [df16.dy[i] == 1 ? uifdyeq1[i]^4 - 3 :
                                -(3 * threshold[i] + threshold[i]^3) * λ_tobit[i]
              for i in 1:DataFrames.nrow(df16)];
```

#### Test of normality

```{julia}
#=
* Generate the scores to use in the LM test
global xlist age female educ blhisp totchr ins
capture drop score*
foreach var in $xlist {
   generate score`var' = gres1*`var'
}
global scores score* gres1 gres2
summarize $scores gres3 gres4 threshold lambda
=#

# Build score_<var> = gres1·<var> for each xlist regressor. Also stash
# `threshold` and `λ_tobit` as df16 columns for `stata_summarize`.
xlist = [:age, :female, :educ, :blhisp, :totchr, :ins]
for v in xlist
    df16[!, Symbol("score_$v")] = df16.gres1 .* Float64.(_rawval.(df16[!, v]))
end
df16.threshold = threshold
df16.lambda    = λ_tobit

scores_vars = [:score_age, :score_female, :score_educ,
               :score_blhisp, :score_totchr, :score_ins,
               :gres1, :gres2]
stata_summarize(df16,
    [scores_vars; :gres3; :gres4; :threshold; :lambda]);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Test of normality in tobit regression
* NR^2 from the uncentered regression has chi-squared distribution
capture drop one
generate one = 1
quietly regress one gres3 gres4 $scores, noconstant
display "N R^2 = " e(N)*e(r2) " with p-value = " chi2tail(2,e(N)*e(r2))
=#

# LM test of normality (Pagan-Vella / Newey style): regress a vector of ones
# on (gres3, gres4, score_*, gres1, gres2) with NO constant. The score
# components are mean-zero by the tobit FOC, so the uncentered R² reflects
# what gres3 and gres4 add. N·R² ~ χ²(2) under H0: normality.
let
    N_h = DataFrames.nrow(df16)
    one = ones(N_h)
    X = hcat(df16.gres3, df16.gres4,
             df16.score_age, df16.score_female, df16.score_educ,
             df16.score_blhisp, df16.score_totchr, df16.score_ins,
             df16.gres1, df16.gres2)
    β = X \ one
    yhat = X * β
    RSS = sum((one .- yhat).^2)
    TSS_uncentered = sum(one.^2)     # = N
    R2_uncentered  = 1 - RSS / TSS_uncentered
    LM = N_h * R2_uncentered
    p  = 1 - cdf(Chisq(2), LM)
    Printf.@printf("N R^2 = %.6f  with p-value = %.6g\n", LM, p)
end;
```

#### Test of homoskedasticity

```{julia}
#=
* Test of homoskedasticity in tobit regression
global xlist age female educ blhisp totchr ins
capture drop score2*
foreach var in $xlist {
   generate score2`var' = gres2*`var'
}
global scores2 score* score2* gres1 gres2
quietly regress one gres3 gres4 $scores2, noconstant
display "N R^2 = " e(N)*e(r2) " with p-value = " chi2tail(2,e(N)*e(r2))
=#

# LM test of homoskedasticity — adds `score2_<var> = gres2·<var>` (the
# σ-equation gradient pieces) on top of the score_<var> block. `gres3` and
# `gres4` remain the test directions; N·R² ~ χ²(2) under H0: homoskedastic.
xlist = [:age, :female, :educ, :blhisp, :totchr, :ins]
for v in xlist
    df16[!, Symbol("score2_$v")] = df16.gres2 .* Float64.(_rawval.(df16[!, v]))
end
let
    N_h = DataFrames.nrow(df16)
    one = ones(N_h)
    X = hcat(df16.gres3, df16.gres4,
             df16.score_age, df16.score_female, df16.score_educ,
             df16.score_blhisp, df16.score_totchr, df16.score_ins,
             df16.score2_age, df16.score2_female, df16.score2_educ,
             df16.score2_blhisp, df16.score2_totchr, df16.score2_ins,
             df16.gres1, df16.gres2)
    β   = X \ one
    yhat = X * β
    RSS = sum((one .- yhat).^2)
    R2_uncentered = 1 - RSS / N_h
    LM = N_h * R2_uncentered
    p  = 1 - cdf(Chisq(2), LM)
    Printf.@printf("N R^2 = %.6f  with p-value = %.6g\n", LM, p)
end;
```

```{julia}
#=
// Following not included in book
* tobcm command is deprecated - use margins to compute marginal effects after tobit
* For our example lny is left-censored at 0- but 0 is a noncensored observation
tobit lny $xlist, ll(0)
margins, dydx(*) predict(ystar(0,.)) atmeans
=#

# Refit tobit on `lny` with explicit left-censoring at 0, then compute the
# at-mean marginal effects on `ystar(0, .) = max(0, y)` (censored expectation).
tobit_lny_ll0 = stata_tobit(df16, :lny,
    [:age, :female, :educ, :blhisp, :totchr, :ins]; ll = 0.0)
stata_margins_tobit(tobit_lny_ll0; predict = :e_uncond, at = :mean);
```

### 16.4.7 Next step?

## 16.5 Two-part model in logs

### 16.5.1 Model structure

### 16.5.2 Part 1 specification

```{julia}
#=
* Part 1 of the two-part model
probit dy $xlist, nolog
scalar llprobit = e(ll)
=#

# Part 1: probit on the 0/1 indicator `dy = 1{ambexp > 0}`. `stata_probit`
# takes a StatsModels `@formula` and prints the Stata-style table. Stash the
# log-likelihood as `llprobit` for the two-part sum-of-LLs check.
probit_part1 = stata_probit(df16,
    @formula(dy ~ age + female + educ + blhisp + totchr + ins))
llprobit = probit_part1.ll
Printf.@printf("llprobit = %.6f\n", llprobit);
```

### 16.5.3 Part 2 of the two-part model

```{julia}
#=
* Part 2 of the two-part model
regress lny $xlist if dy==1
scalar lllognormal = e(ll)
predict rlambexp, residuals
=#

# Part 2: OLS of `lny` on `$xlist` restricted to the dy == 1 subsample.
# `stata_ols_fit` fits once via FixedEffectModels, prints the Stata table,
# and returns residuals + OLS log-likelihood (Stata's `e(ll)`).
df16_pos  = DataFrames.filter(:dy => ==(1), df16)
ols_part2 = stata_ols_fit(df16_pos, :lny,
    [:age, :female, :educ, :blhisp, :totchr, :ins])
df16_pos.rlambexp = ols_part2.residuals
lllognormal       = ols_part2.ll
Printf.@printf("lllognormal = %.6f   (n = %d, σ̂² = %.6f)\n",
               lllognormal, ols_part2.n, ols_part2.σ̂²)

# Stata's `predict rlambexp, residuals` writes residuals for ALL rows (using
# the fitted β on the imputed-zero `lny` values for dy==0 obs), so downstream
# `sktest rlambexp` reads the full-sample residual vector.
df16.rlambexp = Float64.(df16.lny) .-
                FixedEffectModels.predict(ols_part2.model, df16);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Create two-part model log likelihood
scalar lltwopart = llprobit + lllognormal  //two-part model log likelihood
display "lltwopart = " lltwopart
=#

# Two-part log-likelihood: sum of Part-1 probit LL and Part-2 lognormal/OLS
# LL, valid because the joint density factorises as f_dy(dy)·f_lny|dy=1.
lltwopart = llprobit + lllognormal
Printf.@printf("lltwopart = %.6f\n", lltwopart);
```

```{julia}
#=
* hettest and sktest commands
quietly regress lny $xlist if dy==1
hettest
sktest rlambexp
=#

# `estat_hettest` — Breusch-Pagan / Cook-Weisberg test against the fitted
# values on the estimation sample (dy == 1). Fit the regression quietly
# (via FixedEffectModels.reg, no printed table) and pass the model.
xs16   = [:age, :female, :educ, :blhisp, :totchr, :ins]
m_hty  = FixedEffectModels.reg(df16_pos, term(:lny) ~ sum(term.(xs16)))
estat_hettest(m_hty, df16_pos)

# `stata_sktest` on the full-sample residual vector (Stata's `predict` writes
# residuals for all rows, including the imputed-zero dy == 0 obs).
stata_sktest(df16, :rlambexp);
```

## 16.6 Selection model

### 16.6.1 Model structure and assumptions

### 16.6.2 ML estimation

### 16.6.3 Estimation without exclusion restrictions

```{julia}
#=
* Heckman MLE without exclusion restrictions
heckman lny $xlist, select(dy = $xlist) nolog
=#

# `stata_heckman` — full-information ML for the sample-selection model. With
# no exclusion restriction the outcome and selection equations share the same
# regressor list. Fits jointly by L-BFGS + ForwardDiff (σ = exp(lnσ), ρ =
# tanh(athrho) reparametrisations), OIM SEs from an FD Hessian with a
# delta-method back to (σ, ρ), and prints Stata's two-equation block plus σ̂,
# ρ̂, λ̂.
heckman_res = stata_heckman(df16,
    @formula(lny ~ age + female + educ + blhisp + totchr + ins),
    @formula(dy  ~ age + female + educ + blhisp + totchr + ins));
```

### 16.6.4 Two-step estimation

```{julia}
#=
* Heckman 2-step without exclusion restrictions
heckman lny $xlist, select(dy = $xlist) twostep
=#

# `stata_heckman_twostep` — Heckman (1979) two-step estimator: probit
# selection on dy, plug the inverse Mills ratio into the outcome equation,
# OLS on the positives, then Greene's first-stage-corrected variance for the
# outcome block. Without exclusion restrictions the equations share the same
# regressors, identified solely off the non-linearity in the Mills term.
heckman_twostep_res = stata_heckman_twostep(df16,
    @formula(lny ~ age + female + educ + blhisp + totchr + ins),
    @formula(dy  ~ age + female + educ + blhisp + totchr + ins));
```

### 16.6.5 Estimation with exclusion restrictions

```{julia}
#=
* Heckman MLE with exclusion restriction
heckman lny $xlist, select(dy = $xlist income) nolog
=#

# Same `stata_heckman`, now with `income` added to the selection equation
# only — the textbook exclusion restriction. ρ is then identified off the
# linear-projection difference rather than only off the Mills-ratio
# non-linearity.
heckman_excl_res = stata_heckman(df16,
    @formula(lny ~ age + female + educ + blhisp + totchr + ins),
    @formula(dy  ~ age + female + educ + blhisp + totchr + ins + income));
```

```{julia}
#=
// Following not included in book
* Heckman 2-step with exclusion restrictions
heckman lny $xlist, select(dy = $xlist income) twostep
=#

# Same `stata_heckman_twostep`, now with `income` added to the selection
# equation only. The exclusion restriction lets ρ be identified off the
# linear-projection difference, so SEs on β̂ and λ̂ are typically tighter.
heckman_twostep_excl = stata_heckman_twostep(df16,
    @formula(lny ~ age + female + educ + blhisp + totchr + ins),
    @formula(dy  ~ age + female + educ + blhisp + totchr + ins + income));
```

## 16.7 Prediction from log-outcome models

### 16.7.1 Predictions from tobit

```{julia}
#=
// The following yields results that differ from book - book is in error
* Prediction from tobit on lny
generate yhat = exp(xb+0.5*sigma^2)*(1-normal((gamma-xb-sigma^2)/sigma))
generate ytrunchat = yhat / (1 - normal(threshold)) if dy==1
summarize y yhat
summarize y yhat ytrunchat if dy==1
=#

# Predict ambexp level from the tobit on lny via `stata_predict_tobit_lognormal`:
#   ŷ_i        = exp(xβ̂ + ½σ²) · (1 − Φ((γ − xβ̂ − σ²)/σ))
#   ŷ_trunc_i  = ŷ_i / (1 − Φ((γ − xβ̂)/σ))           for dy_i = 1
#
# Two Stata-session quirks reproduced here:
#   1. `$xlist` undefined ⇒ intercept-only tobit ⇒ `xb` = α̂ ≈ 5.519 (the
#      unconditional mean of lny), so `yhat` is the same for every row.
#   2. Newer Stata stores `var(e.lny) = σ̂²` where the textbook reads σ, so
#      the yhat formula substitutes σ ← σ̂² and the correction explodes — the
#      "book is in error" pathology.
sigma_buggy_lny = tobit_lny_res.σ^2          # what Stata's `sigma` carries
df16.yhat       = stata_predict_tobit_lognormal(tobit_lny_res;
                                                σ = sigma_buggy_lny,
                                                γ = γ_amb)
ytrunchat_all   = stata_predict_tobit_lognormal(tobit_lny_res;
                                                σ = sigma_buggy_lny,
                                                γ = γ_amb,
                                                truncated = true)
df16.ytrunchat  = [df16.dy[i] == 1 ? ytrunchat_all[i] : missing
                   for i in 1:DataFrames.nrow(df16)]

# `summarize y yhat` over the FULL sample.
stata_summarize(df16, [:y, :yhat])

# `summarize y yhat ytrunchat if dy==1` over the positive subsample.
stata_summarize(DataFrames.filter(:dy => ==(1), df16),
                [:y, :yhat, :ytrunchat]);
```

```{julia}
#=
// Following not included in book
* THE FOLLOWING SHOWS THAT THE POOR PREDICTIONS ARE DUE TO HIGH SIGMA
scalar sigma = 1
generate yhatsigma1 = exp(xb+0.5*sigma^2)*(1-normal((gamma-xb-sigma^2)/sigma))
scalar sigma = 2
generate yhatsigma2 = exp(xb+0.5*sigma^2)*(1-normal((gamma-xb-sigma^2)/sigma))
summarize yhat*
summarize yhat* if dy==1
=#

# σ-sensitivity check. The main `yhat` exploded because Stata's `sigma`
# scalar held σ̂² (≈ 7.39). Plugging σ = 1 and σ = 2 (closer to the
# OLS-on-positives RMSE ≈ 1.27) tames the correction — showing how much of
# the pathology is driven by σ alone, with intercept-only `xb` ≈ 5.519 fixed.
df16.yhatsigma1 = stata_predict_tobit_lognormal(tobit_lny_res; σ = 1.0, γ = γ_amb)
df16.yhatsigma2 = stata_predict_tobit_lognormal(tobit_lny_res; σ = 2.0, γ = γ_amb)

# `summarize yhat*` over the full sample.
stata_summarize(df16, [:yhat, :yhatsigma1, :yhatsigma2])

# `summarize yhat* if dy==1` over the positive subsample.
stata_summarize(DataFrames.filter(:dy => ==(1), df16),
                [:yhat, :yhatsigma1, :yhatsigma2]);
```

### 16.7.2 Predictions from two-part model

```{julia}
#=
* Two-part model predictions
quietly probit dy $xlist
predict dyhat, pr
quietly regress lny $xlist if dy==1
predict xbpos, xb
generate  yhatpos = exp(xbpos+0.5*e(rmse)^2)

* Unconditional prediction from two-part model
generate yhat2step = dyhat*yhatpos
summarize yhat2step y
summarize yhatpos y if dy==1
=#

# Two-part model predictions reusing the fits from §16.5.2 / §16.5.3.
#   dyhat_i    = Φ(x_i' γ̂)               (probit, Part 1)
#   xbpos_i   = x_i' β̂_part2              (OLS on positives, applied to all rows)
#   yhatpos_i = exp(xbpos_i + ½·rmse²)    (lognormal mean; rmse² = σ̂²_unbiased)
#   yhat2step = dyhat · yhatpos           (unconditional two-part)
df16.dyhat   = GLM.predict(probit_part1.model, df16)
df16.xbpos   = FixedEffectModels.predict(ols_part2.model, df16)

# Stata's `e(rmse) = sqrt(RSS / (N − k))` — the *unbiased* root-MSE.
let k = length(ols_part2.β)
    rmse² = sum(ols_part2.residuals.^2) / (ols_part2.n - k)
    df16.yhatpos    = exp.(df16.xbpos .+ 0.5 .* rmse²)
end
df16.yhat2step = df16.dyhat .* df16.yhatpos

# `summarize yhat2step y` over the full sample.
stata_summarize(df16, [:yhat2step, :y])

# `summarize yhatpos y if dy==1` over the positive subsample.
stata_summarize(DataFrames.filter(:dy => ==(1), df16),
                [:yhatpos, :y]);
```

### 16.7.3 Predictions from selection model

```{julia}
#=
* Heckman model predictions
quietly heckman lny $xlist, select(dy = $xlist)
predict probpos, psel
predict x1b1, xbsel
predict x2b2, xb
scalar sig2sq = e(sigma)^2
scalar sig12sq = e(rho)*e(sigma)^2
display "sigma1sq = 1" " sigma12sq = " sig12sq " sigma2sq = " sig2sq
generate yhatheck = exp(x2b2 + 0.5*(sig2sq))*(1 - normal(-x1b1-sig12sq))
generate yhatposheck = yhatheck/probpos
summarize yhatheck y probpos dy
summarize yhatposheck probpos dy y if dy==1
=#

# `stata_predict_heckman` — Stata `heckman` post-estimation predictions from
# the no-exclusion fit `heckman_res` (§16.6.3):
#   probpos = Φ(z'γ̂), x1b1 = z'γ̂, x2b2 = x'β̂
#   sig2sq  = σ̂², sig12sq = ρ̂σ̂² (textbook scalar; σ² is the textbook bug)
#   yhatheck    = exp(x2b2 + ½σ²)·(1 − Φ(−x1b1 − sig12sq))
#   yhatposheck = yhatheck / probpos
heck_pred = stata_predict_heckman(df16, heckman_res)
df16.probpos     = heck_pred.probpos
df16.x1b1        = heck_pred.x1b1
df16.x2b2        = heck_pred.x2b2
df16.yhatheck    = heck_pred.yhatheck
df16.yhatposheck = heck_pred.yhatposheck

Printf.@printf("sigma1sq = 1 sigma12sq = %.7g sigma2sq = %.7g\n",
               heck_pred.sig12sq, heck_pred.sig2sq)

# `summarize yhatheck y probpos dy` over the full sample.
stata_summarize(df16, [:yhatheck, :y, :probpos, :dy])

# `summarize yhatposheck probpos dy y if dy==1` over the positives.
stata_summarize(DataFrames.filter(:dy => ==(1), df16),
                [:yhatposheck, :probpos, :dy, :y]);
```

```{julia}
#=
// Following not included in book
* THE FOLLOWING SHOWS THAT THE POOR PREDICTIONS ARE DUE TO HIGH SIGMA
scalar sigma = 1
generate yhatsigma1 = exp(xb+0.5*sigma^2)*(1-normal((gamma-xb-sigma^2)/sigma))
scalar sigma = 2
generate yhatsigma2 = exp(xb+0.5*sigma^2)*(1-normal((gamma-xb-sigma^2)/sigma))
summarize yhat*
summarize yhat* if dy==1
=#

# Final wrap-up `summarize yhat*` — all §16.7 predictions together. The three
# constant columns (yhat, yhatsigma1, yhatsigma2) share the intercept-only
# tobit `xb` ≈ 5.519; only `yhat` carries the buggy `sigma = σ̂²` substitution.
df16.yhatsigma1 = stata_predict_tobit_lognormal(tobit_lny_res; σ = 1.0, γ = γ_amb)
df16.yhatsigma2 = stata_predict_tobit_lognormal(tobit_lny_res; σ = 2.0, γ = γ_amb)

# `summarize yhat*` — Stata expands `yhat*` in variable-creation order.
yhat_cols = [:yhat, :yhatpos, :yhat2step,
             :yhatheck, :yhatposheck,
             :yhatsigma1, :yhatsigma2]
stata_summarize(df16, yhat_cols)

# `summarize yhat* if dy==1` — same set on the positive subsample.
stata_summarize(DataFrames.filter(:dy => ==(1), df16), yhat_cols);
```

## 16.8 Stata resources

## 16.9 Exercises
